content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

September 17, 1976


Jessie Ramming, Principal
Casmalia School District
Post Office Box R
Casmalia, California  93429

Dear Ms. Ramming:

     Senator Rains has provided our agency with a copy of the
resolution adopted by the board of your school district on
August 5, 1976.  Essentially, the resolution asks the legislature to amend the Political Reform Act of 1974 to exempt
elected school board members from the financial disclosure
provisions of Government Code Sections 87100, et seq.

     It is understandable that unpaid school board members
believe it is an imposition to be compelled to disclose certain
of their private financial interests.  However, under existing
law, school districts are governmental agencies; members of
boards of education as well as certain administrative personnel
within the school districts "make or participate in the making
of governmental decisions" within the meaning of the Political
Reform Act; and many of their decisions affect private financial
interests.  Under these circumstances, the Act requires that
school districts adopt Conflict of Interest Codes applicable
to members and officials who are capable of affecting such
financial interests.  Only those financial interests which
could be affected by the filer's official actions must be
disclosed and it is the school district which has the first
and primary responsibility to identify these potential conflicts
of interest and provide for disclosure.  Hopefully,
individuals who object to overly broad disclosure will take an
active role in the design of each code's disclosure requirements, so that intrusion of personal privacy will be kept at a
minimum consistent with the objective of preventing or disclosing conflicts of interest.

     The Fair Political Practices Commission is required to
and has provided technical assistance to state and local agencies
in drafting codes.  In particular, we have urged agencies to
avoid the easy path of imposing disclosure requirements with a
broad brush but rather to describe with as much precision as
possible the particular types of financial interests which
could be affected by official decisions and which therefore
must be disclosed.

TECHNICAL ASSISTANCE/ADMINISTRATION (916) 322-5660 • EXECUTIVE/LEGAL 322-5901 • ENFORCEMENT/CONFLICT OF INTEREST 322-6441


Jessie Ramming
September 17, 1976
Page Two


     The Political Reform Act was, of course, passed as an
initiative. Section 81012(a) of the Act provides that the Act
may be amended to further its purposes. Among those purposes
are the following:

          Assets and income of public officials which
          may be materially affected by their official
          actions should be disclosed and in appropriate
          circumstances the officials should be dis-
          qualified from acting in order that conflicts
          of interest may be avoided.

                         Gov. Code § 81002(g).

     Given the circumstances, it is doubtful that the exemption of
school board members from the requirements of the Act could be
construed to be in furtherance of the purposes of the Act.

     Even assuming, however, that your proposal could be adopted
by the Legislature, and fully recognizing the right of school
board members to express their views, I believe the proposal
is without merit. School boards make decisions of great con-
sequence to their communities. Like other public officials,
school board members serve in a position of public trust, and
the people have the right to be assured that their decisions
will be made free from bias caused by their own financial
interests. Disclosure of any financial interests which could
foreseeably be affected by their official actions is a reason-
able and effective means of preventing conflicts of interest.
In recent years the California Supreme Court and the courts of
many other states have ruled that such disclosure requirements
are valid and that any consequent intrusion of the privacy of
the official is justified by the important public interest
that is served.

     I hope that this information is helpful. If you have
further questions, please call Mr. Delbert Spurlock, Chief of
our Conflicts of Interest Division, at (916) 322-6444.

                              Sincerely,

                              [signature]

                              Michael Bennett
                              Executive Director

MB:plh

cc:   Senator Omer Rains


MEMBERS
OMER L. RAINES
CHAIRMAN

H. L. RICHARDSON
VICE CHAIRMAN

DENNIS E. CARPENTER
ALAN P. GARCIA

BILL GREENE

ARLEN GREGORIO

NATE HOLDEN

COMMITTEE ADDRESS
ROOM 5090, STATE CAPITOL
SACRAMENTO, CA 95814
(916) 445-2601

JAMES A. ELEDWING, JR.
CONSULTANT

SUSAN C. TONINI
COMMITTEE SECRETARY

California Legislature

SENATE COMMITTEE ON
ELECTIONS AND REAPPORTIONMENT

OMER L. RAINS
CHAIRMAN

September 10, 1976

Jessie Ramming
Principal
Casmalia School District
Post Office Box K
Casmalia, California 93429

Dear Ms. Ramming:

Thank you for sending me a copy of your school board's resolution,
expressing the view that local school board members should be
excluded from the conflict of interest provisions of the Political
Reform Act.

The Act, at Section 87300, requires agencies to adopt and
promulgate a conflict of interest code. "Agency" at Section 82003
includes "local government agency". "Local government agency"
includes "school district". Section 82041.

I sympathize with your view that these provisions are burdensome
on persons serving, in many cases, without any remuneration at all.
I assume, however, the drafters of the initiative (and perhaps the
public in voting for the initiative) perceived the real threat of
conflicts of interest in awarding contracts in the school district
as well as any other legislative or administrative body. In fact,
it is arguable, that since such a large percentage of a school
board's business is awarding contracts, such boards should be
included.

In any event, since the Political Reform Act was an initiative
measure, it can only be amended by a vote of the people, except an
amendment "to further its purposes" could be passed by a two-thirds
vote of each house of the Legislature. A question arises as to
whether such an amendment would be construed to "further its purposes".
My staff is reviewing your suggestion and an appropriate remedy.

n~ ~
0 di [illegible] ~

Jessie Ramming                    2                    September 10, 1976


For the views of the Fair Political Practices Commission, I am
forwarding this letter to Robert Stern, general counsel.

Very truly yours,


OMER L. RAINS

OLR/jak

cc: Robert Stern, General Counsel, Fair Political Practices
    Commission, 925 J Street, Suite 522, Sacramento  95814


[Content is not legible enough to transcribe accurately]

CASMALIA SCHOOL DISTRICT
POST OFFICE BOX K
CASMALIA, CALIFORNIA 93429

September 7, 1976

State Senator Omer Mains
State Capitol
Sacramento, Calif. 95814

Dear Sir,

IT IS HEREBY RESOLVED by the Board of Education of the
Casmalia School District to publicly state its opposi-
tion to the inclusion of elected public school board
members within the requirements of the California "Po-
litical Reform Act of 1974" and it is resolved to urge
its legislators to take the necessary action to bring
about the changes needed to exclude school board mem-
bers from the requirements of the California "Political
Reform Act of 1974" and it is resolved to send this re-
solution to the State Legislature.

AYES:     Harp, Jackson and Postiff

NOES:     None

ABSENT:   None

I hereby certify that the foregoing is a true and correct
transcript of a resolution duly adopted and affirmed by
a formal vote of the members of the Governing Board of
the Casmalia School District at a duly constituted regu-
lar meeting of the Board which was open to the public on
August 5, 1976, as it appears upon the minutes of the said
meeting of the said Governing Board.

[signature]
Jessie Manning
Principal and Secretary to
the Board
